content: If $A$ is a square matrix such that $A^2=A$, then $(I-A)^3$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{A}^2=\mathrm{A} . & (\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})^3=(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})\left(\mathrm{I}-2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{A}^2\right) \\ & =(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})(\mathrm{I}-2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{A}) \\ & =(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A}) \\ & =\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A}^2=\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A} \end{array} $$

content: If $A$ and $B$ are two matrices such that $A B$ is an identity matrix and the order of matrix $B$ is $3 \times 4$, then the order of matrix $A$ is
explanation:
Let’s find the size of $A$ step by step:

B has size $3\times4$.

For $A\,B$ to be defined, if $A$ is $m\times n$, then $n$ must equal 3.

So $A$ is $m\times3$, and

$ A\,B\quad\text{has size}\quad (m\times3)\,(3\times4)=(m\times4). $

Since $A\,B$ is an identity matrix, it must be square. Hence $m=4$.

Therefore

$ A\text{ is }4\times3. $

Answer: Option C, $4\times3$.

content: If a matrix $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]$ satisfies $A^6=k A^{\prime}$, then the value of $k$ is
explanation:
$$ A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & A^2=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ & A^3=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 4 & 4 \\ 4 & 4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2^2 & 2^2 \\ 2^2 & 2^2 \end{array}\right] \\ & A^4=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2^3 & 2^3 \\ 2^3 & 2^3 \end{array}\right] \\ & A^6=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2^5 & 2^5 \\ 2^5 & 2^5 \end{array}\right]=2^5\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow 2^5=32 \\ & \therefore k=32 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}k & 2 \\ 2 & k\end{array}\right]$ and $\left|A^3\right|=125$, then the value of $k$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} k & 2 \\ 2 & k \end{array}\right] \\ & |A|=k^2-4 \\ & |A|^3=|A| \cdot|A| \cdot|A|=125, \text { given } \\ & \Rightarrow\left(k^2-4\right)^3=125 \\ & \Rightarrow k^2-4=5 \Rightarrow k^2=9 \\ & \Rightarrow k= \pm 3 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A$ is a square matrix satisfying the equation $A^2-5 A+7 I=0$, where $I$ is the $I$ dentity matrix and 0 is null matrix of same order, then $A^{-1}=$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Given }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}^2-5 \mathrm{~A}+7 \mathrm{I}=0\left(\because \text { Multiply by } \mathrm{A}^{-1} \text { both sides }|\mathrm{A}| \neq 0\right) \\ & \mathrm{A}-5 \mathrm{I}+7 \mathrm{~A}^{-1}=0 \\ & \mathrm{~A}^{-1}=\frac{(5 \mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})}{7} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $A$ is a square matrix of order $3 \times 3, \operatorname{det} A=3$, then the value of $\operatorname{det}\left(3 A^{-1}\right)$ is
explanation:
First note for a 3×3 matrix $A$ with $\det A=3$:

$\det(A^{-1})=\frac{1}{\det A}=\frac13.$

When you multiply a 3×3 matrix by a scalar $3$, its determinant is multiplied by $3^3$. So

$ \det\bigl(3A^{-1}\bigr) =3^3\;\det(A^{-1}) =27\;\times\frac13 =9. $

Hence the correct choice is Option D: 9.

content: If $B=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 3 \\ 1 & \alpha\end{array}\right]$ be the adjoint of a matrix $A$ and $|A|=2$, then the value of $\alpha$ is
explanation:
Let $B=\operatorname{adj}(A)$ for a $2\times2$ matrix $A=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}$. Recall that for $2\times2$,

adj$(A)=\begin{pmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{pmatrix}.$

Here we’re given

$ \displaystyle B=\begin{pmatrix}1&3\\1&\alpha\end{pmatrix}.$

By matching entries:

• $d=1$

• $-b=3\implies b=-3$

• $-c=1\implies c=-1$

• $a=\alpha$

So

$ A=\begin{pmatrix}\alpha & -3\\-1 & 1\end{pmatrix}, \quad |A| = \alpha\cdot1 -(-3)(-1) = \alpha - 3. $

Since $|A|=2$, we get

$ \alpha - 3 = 2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \alpha = 5. $

Answer: 5.

content: If $A$ is a square matrix, such that $A^2=A$, then $(I+A)^3$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} (I+A)^3 & =I^3+A^3+3 I^2 A+3 A^2 I \\ & =I+A^3+3 A+3 A^2 \\ & =I+A^2 \cdot A+3 A+3 A \quad\left[\because A^2=A\right] \\ & =I+A \cdot A+6 A \\ & =I+A^2+6 A \\ & =I+A+6 A \quad\left[\because A^2=A\right] \\ & =I+7 A \end{aligned}$$

content: If $A=\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right)$, then $A^{10}$ is equal to
explanation:
$\because\quad A^2=\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right)$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad A^2 & =\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1+1 & 1+1 \\ 1+1 & 1+1 \end{array}\right) \\ & =\left(\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{array}\right)=2\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right)=2 A \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad A^3 & =\left(\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right) \\ & =\left(\begin{array}{ll} 2+2 & 2+2 \\ 2+2 & 2+2 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{ll} 4 & 4 \\ 4 & 4 \end{array}\right) \\ & =4\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right)=4 A \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Hence, } A^{10}=2^9 A\\ &\left[\because A^n=2^{n-1} A\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: If $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x-3 & 2 x^2-18 & 2 x^3-81 \\ x-5 & 2 x^2-50 & 4 x^3-500 \\ 1 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right|$, then $f(\mathrm{l}) \cdot f(3)+f(3) \cdot f(5)+f(5) \cdot f(\mathrm{l})$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { } \begin{aligned} f(x)=2(x-5)\left[\left.\begin{array}{ccc} x-3 & x^2-9 & 2 x^3-81 \\ 1 & x+5 & 4\left(x^2+5 x+25\right) \\ 1 & 1 \end{array} \right\rvert\,\right. \\ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow f(5) & =0 \\ \text { and } f(1) & =2(-4)\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -8 & -79 \\ 1 & 6 & 124 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \end{array}\right] \\ & =-8[212-968+395]=2888 \\ f(3) & =2(-2)\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & -27 \\ 1 & 8 & 196 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \end{array}\right] \\ & =-4[0+0-27(1-8)]=-756 \\ \text { So, } f(1) & \cdot f(3)+f(3) \cdot f(5)+f(5) \cdot f(1) \\ & =2888 \times(-756)+0+0=-2183328 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} . \end{aligned}\\ &\text { No option matched. } \end{aligned}$$

content: If $P=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & \alpha & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 4\end{array}\right]$ is the adjoint of a $3 \times 3$ $\operatorname{matrix} A$ and $|A|=4$, then $\alpha$ is equal to
explanation:
$P=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & \alpha & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 4\end{array}\right]$

For $3 \times 3$, matrix,

$$\begin{aligned} & |\operatorname{adj} A|=|A|^2 \\ & |\operatorname{adj} A|=(4)^2=16 \end{aligned}$$

Now, $P=\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & \alpha & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 4\end{array}\right|=16$

$$\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow & 1(12-12)-\alpha(4-6)+3(4-6) =16 \\ \Rightarrow & 0+2 \alpha-6 =16 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \alpha =22 \end{array}$$

Hence, $\alpha=11$

content: If $A=\left|\begin{array}{cc}x & 1 \\ 1 & x\end{array}\right|$ and $B=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & x & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & x\end{array}\right|$, then $\frac{d B}{d x}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} B & =\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & x & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & x \end{array}\right| \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & x & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & x \end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & x \end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll} x & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & x & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right| \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left(x^2-1\right)+\left(x^2-1\right)+\left(x^2-1\right) \\ & =3\left(x^2-1\right) \\ & =3 A\quad \left[\because A=\left|\begin{array}{ll} x & 1 \\ 1 & x \end{array}\right|=x^2-1\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\text { The value of }\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \sin ^2 14^{\circ} & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} & \tan 135^{\circ} \\ \sin ^2 66^{\circ} & \tan 135^{\circ} & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} \\ \tan 135^{\circ} & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} \end{array}\right|$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text {Here, }\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \sin ^2 14^{\circ} & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} & \tan 135^{\circ} \\ \sin ^2 66^{\circ} & \tan 135^{\circ} & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} \\ \tan 135^{\circ} & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} \end{array}\right| \\ & \text { Applying } C_1 \rightarrow C_1+C_2+C_3 \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{lll} \sin ^2 14^{\circ}+\sin ^2 66^{\circ}+\tan 135^{\circ} & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} \tan 135^{\circ} \\ =\sin ^2 14^{\circ}+\sin ^2 66^{\circ}+\tan 135^{\circ} & \tan 135^{\circ} \sin ^2 14^{\circ} \\ \sin ^2 14^{\circ}+\sin ^2 66^{\circ}+\tan 135^{\circ} & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} \sin ^2 66^{\circ} \end{array}\right| \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sin ^2 14^{\circ}+\sin ^2 66^{\circ}+\tan 135^{\circ} \\ & \left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} & \tan 135^{\circ} \\ 1 & \tan 135^{\circ} & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} \\ 1 & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} \end{array}\right| \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sin ^2 14^{\circ}+\sin ^2 66^{\circ}-1\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & \sin ^2 66 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} \\ 1 & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} \end{array}\right| . \\ & \text { Applying } R_1 \rightarrow R_1-R_2, R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_3 \\ & =\cos ^2 66^{\circ}+\sin ^2 66^{\circ}-1 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 1+\sin ^2 66^{\circ} & -1-\sin ^2 14^{\circ} \\ 0 & -1-\sin ^2 14^{\circ} & \sin ^2 14^{\circ}-\sin ^2 66^{\circ} \\ 1 & \sin ^2 14^{\circ} & \sin ^2 66^{\circ} \end{array}\right| \\ & \left(\cos ^2 66^{\circ}+\sin ^2 66^{\circ}-1\right)=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x\left[\begin{array}{l}3 \\ 2\end{array}\right]+y\left[\begin{array}{r}1 \\ -1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}15 \\ 5\end{array}\right]$$, then the value of $$x$$ and $$y$$ are
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{gathered} x\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]+y\left[\begin{array}{c} 1 \\ -1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{r} 15 \\ 5 \end{array}\right] \\ {\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 x \\ 2 x \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} y \\ -y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 15 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]} \\ {\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 x+y \\ 2 x-y \end{array}\right]=\left[\frac{15}{5}\right]} \\ 3 x+y=15 \quad \text{..... (i)}\\ 2 x-y=5 \quad \text{..... (ii)} \end{gathered}$$

Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$5 x=20 \Rightarrow x=4$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{So,} \quad & y=15-3 x \quad [\text{From Eq. (i)}]\\ & y=15-3 \times 4=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$A$$ and $$B$$ are two matrices, such that $$A B=B$$ and $$B A=A$$, then $$A^2+B^2$$ equals to
explanation:
Given, $$A B=B$$ and $$B A=A$$

$$\begin{aligned} A^2+B^2 & =A A+B B=A(B A)+B(A B) \\ & =(A B)(A)+(B A) B=B A+A B=A+B \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2-k & 2 \\ 1 & 3-k\end{array}\right]$$ is singular matrix, then the value of $$5 k-k^2$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2-k & 2 \\ 1 & 3-k\end{array}\right]$$ is singular matrix we know that $$A$$ is singular matrix, then $$|A|=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & |A|=(2-k)(3-k)-2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 0=6-2 k-3 k+k^2-2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k^2-5 k+4=0 \Rightarrow k^2-5 k=-4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 k-k^2=4 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2\end{array}\right|$$ and $$\Delta_1=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ b c & c a & a b \\ a & b & c\end{array}\right|$$, then
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2 \end{array}\right| \\ \text { and } \Delta_1 & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ b c & c a & a b \\ a & b & c \end{array}\right| \\ \Delta & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2 \end{array}\right| \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} R_1 \rightarrow R_1-R_2 \text { and } R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_3 & \\ \Delta & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & a-b & a^2-b^2 \\ 0 & b-c & b^2-c^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2 \end{array}\right| \\ \Delta & =(a-b)(b-c)\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 1 & a+b \\ 0 & 1 & b+c \\ 1 & c & c^2 \end{array}\right| \\ \Delta & =(a-b)(b-c)(c-a) \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Now, } \Delta_1 & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ b c & c a & a b \\ a & b & c \end{array}\right| \\ C_1 \rightarrow C_1 & -C_2, C_2 \rightarrow C_2-C_3 \\ \Delta_1 & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ c(b-a) & a(c-b) & a b \\ a-b & b-c & c \end{array}\right| \\ \Delta_1 & =(a-b)(b-c)\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -c & -a & a b \\ 1 & 1 & c \end{array}\right| \\ \Delta_1 & =(a-b)(b-c)(a-c) \\ \Delta_1 & =-(a-b)(b-c)(c-a) \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\Delta=-\Delta_1$$

content: If $$A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & \tan \alpha / 2 \\ -\tan \alpha / 2 & 1\end{array}\right]$$ and $$A B=I$$, then $$B$$ is equal to
explanation:
Given, $$A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & \tan \frac{\alpha}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\alpha}{2} & 1\end{array}\right]$$ and $$A B=I$$

$$\Rightarrow B=A^{-1}$$

Now $$A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & \tan \frac{\alpha}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\alpha}{2} & 1\end{array}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} & |A|=\left[\begin{array}{cr} 1 & \tan \frac{\alpha}{2} \\ -\tan \frac{\alpha}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]=1+\operatorname{an}^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}=\sec ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2} \\ & \operatorname{adj} A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan \frac{\alpha}{2} \\ \tan \frac{\alpha}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$A^{-1}=\frac{1}{\sec ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\tan \frac{\alpha}{2} \\ \tan \frac{\alpha}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]$$

$$ = {\cos ^2}{\alpha \over 2}\left[ {\matrix{ 1 & { - \tan {\alpha \over 2}} \cr {\tan {\alpha \over 2}} & 1 \cr } } \right] = {\cos ^2}{\alpha \over 2}AT$$

$$B=\left[\cos ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}\right] A T$$

content: If $$A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & -1 \\ 3 & -2\end{array}\right]$$, then the inverse of the matrix $$A^3$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right] \\ & |A|=-4+3=-1 \\ & \operatorname{adj}(A)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} -2 & -3 \\ -(-1) & 2 \end{array}\right]^T=\left[\begin{array}{cc} -2 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]^T=\left[\begin{array}{ll} -2 & 1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ & A^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj}(A)}{|A|}=\frac{\left[\begin{array}{ll} -2 & 1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{array}\right]}{(-1)}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & -1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]=A \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & A^{-1} & =A \Rightarrow A \cdot A^{-1}=A \cdot A \\ \Rightarrow & & I & =A^2 \Rightarrow A \cdot I=A \cdot A^2 \\ \Rightarrow & & A & =A^3 \Rightarrow(A)^{-1}=\left(A^3\right)^{-1} \\ \Rightarrow & & A & =\left(A^3\right)^{-1} \Rightarrow\left(A^3\right)^{-1}=A \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$A$$ is a skew symmetric matrix, then A$$^{2021}$$ is
explanation:
Given, $$A^T=-A$$

Let $$\quad P=A^{2021}$$

$$\begin{aligned} P^T & =\left[A^{2021}\right]^T=\left[A^T\right]^{2021} \\ & =[-A]^{2021}=-[A]^{2021}=-P \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $$A^{2021}$$ is also a skew symmetric matrix.

content: If $$A$$ is a $$3 \times 3$$ matrix such that $$|5 \cdot \operatorname{adj} A|=5$$, then $$|A|$$ is equal to
explanation:
Given, $$A$$ is a $$3 \times 3$$ matrix and $$|5 \cdot \operatorname{Adj}(A)|=5$$

$$\begin{aligned} |5 \cdot \operatorname{adj}(A)| & =5 \Rightarrow 5^3|\operatorname{adj}(A)|=5 \\ \Rightarrow \quad|\operatorname{adj}(A)| & =\frac{1}{5^2} \Rightarrow|A|^{3-1}=\frac{1}{5^2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad|A|^2 & =\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2 \Rightarrow|A|= \pm \frac{1}{5} \end{aligned}$$

content:
If there are two values of '$$a$$' which makes determinant

$$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 5 \\ 2 & a & -1 \\ 0 & 4 & 2 a \end{array}\right|=86$$

Then, the sum of these number is
explanation:
$$\text { Given, } \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 5 \\ 2 & a & -1 \\ 0 & 4 & 2 a \end{array}\right|=86 \Rightarrow$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -2 & 5 \\ 2 & a & -1 \\ 0 & 4 & 2 a \end{array}\right|=86 \\ & \Rightarrow 1\left[2 a^2+4\right]+2[4 a+0]+5[8-0]=86 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 a^2+4+8 a+40=86 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 a^2+8 a-42=0 \Rightarrow a^2+4 a-21=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a^2+7 a-3 a-21=0 \Rightarrow(a+7)(a-3)=0 \\ & a=-7,3 \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Required sum $$=-7+3=-4$$

content: If $$A_n=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1-n & n \\ n & 1-n\end{array}\right]$$, then $$\left|A_1\right|+\left|A_2\right|+\ldots .\left|A_{2021}\right|=$$
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} A_n & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1-n & n \\ n & 1-n \end{array}\right] \text { and } \\ \left|A_n\right| & =\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1-n & n \\ n & 1-n \end{array}\right] \\ & =(1-n)^2-n^2=1+n^2-2 n-n^2 \\ \left|A_n\right| & =1-2 n \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Now, }&\left|A_1\right|+\left|A_2\right|+\left|A_3\right|+\ldots .+\left|A_{2021}\right| \\ & =(1-2)+(1-4)+(1-6)+\ldots+(1-4042) \\ & =(1+1+\ldots .+1)-(2+4+6+\ldots .+4042) \\ & =2021-\left[\frac{2021}{2}(2+4042)\right] \\ & =2021-\frac{2021}{2} \times 4044=2021-2021 \times 2022 \\ & =2021(1-2022)=-(2021)^2 \end{aligned}$$

content:

content: Let $$M$$ be $$2 \times 2$$ symmetric matrix with integer entries, then $$M$$ is invertible if

options.content: the first column of $$M$$ is the transpose of second row of $$M$$.
the second row of $$M$$ is the transpose of first column of $$M$$.
$$M$$ is diagonal matrix with non-zero entries in the principal diagonal.
The product of entries in the principal diagonal of $$M$$ is the product of entries in the other diagonal.

explanation:
Let a symmetric matrix

$$M=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & c \\ c & b \end{array}\right]$$

For matrix to be invertible, determinant must not be equal to zero.

$$\begin{aligned} & |M|=a b-c^2 \neq 0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad a b & \neq c^2 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $$M$$ is a diagonal matrix with non-zero entries in the main diagonal and the product of entries in the main diagonal of $$M$$ is not the square of an integer.

content: The inverse of the matrix $$\left[\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 3\end{array}\right]$$ is
explanation:
Key Idea Firstly write the given matrix in the form of $$A=I A$$ then apply sequence of elementary row operation on $$A$$ of LHS to convert it into identity matrix and same operations apply on $$I$$ of RHS

$$\text { Let } A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]$$

For applying row operations $$A=I A$$, where $$A$$ is an identity matrix of order 3.

i.e. $$\left[\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 3\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right] A$$

On applying $$R_1 \rightarrow \frac{R_1}{2}$$, we get

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 5 / 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} \frac{1}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { On applying } R_3 \rightarrow R_3+R_1, \\ & \qquad\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 5 / 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 5 / 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 / 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 / 2 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A \end{aligned}$$

On applying $$R_1 \rightarrow R_1-\frac{5}{2} R_2, R_3 \rightarrow R_3-\frac{5}{2} R_2$$, we get

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -5 / 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 / 2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 / 2 & -5 / 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 / 2 & -5 / 2 & 1 \end{array}\right] A$$

On applying $$R_3 \rightarrow 2 R_3$$, we get

$$\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -5 / 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 / 2 & -5 / 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & -5 & 2 \end{array}\right] A$$

$$R_1 \rightarrow R_1+\frac{5}{2} R_3, R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_3$$, we get

$$\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -15 & 5 \\ -1 & 6 & -2 \\ 1 & -5 & 2 \end{array}\right] A$$

Hence, $$A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}3 & -15 & 5 \\ -1 & 6 & -2 \\ 1 & -5 & 2\end{array}\right]$$

content: If $$P$$ and $$Q$$ are symmetric matrices of the same order then $$P Q-Q P$$ is

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2\end{array}\right]$, then $A^n=2^k A$, where $k$ is equal to
explanation:
Given, $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2\end{array}\right]$

    Now,

    $$ A^2=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{array}\right] $$

    $$ \Rightarrow \quad A^2=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 8 & -8 \\ -8 & 8 \end{array}\right] $$

    $$ \begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & A^2=2^2\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ \Rightarrow & A^2=2^2 A \end{array} $$

    Now, $\quad A^3=A^2 \cdot A$

    $$ \begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & A^3=2^2 \cdot A^2 \\ \Rightarrow & A^3=2^4 \cdot A \\ \Rightarrow & A^4=A^3 \cdot A \\ \Rightarrow & A^4=2^4 \cdot A^2 \\ \Rightarrow & A^4=2^6 A \\ \Rightarrow & A^5=A^4 \cdot A \\ \Rightarrow & A^5=2^6 A^2 \\ & A^5=2^8 A \\ \therefore & A^n=2^{2(n-1)} A \end{array} $$

    Hence, $k=2(n-1)$

content: If $\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}x \\ y\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}2 \\ 4\end{array}\right]$, then the values of $x$ and $y$ respectively are
explanation:
To find the values of $ x $ and $ y $, we start with the matrix equation:

$ \left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 4 \end{array}\right] $

We can solve for the vector $\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]$ using the inverse of the coefficient matrix:

$ \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]^{-1}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 4 \end{array}\right] $

The inverse of the matrix $\left[\begin{array}{cc} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]$ is given by:

$ \left[\begin{array}{cc} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{ad-bc}\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right] $

For our matrix $\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]$, the determinant $ ad-bc = (1)(1) - (1)(-1) = 2 $.

Thus, the inverse is:

$ \frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right] $

Substitute the inverse into the expression for $\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]$:

$ \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 4 \end{array}\right] $

Calculate the matrix product:

$ \frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{c} (1)(2) + (-1)(4) \\ (1)(2) + (1)(4) \end{array}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 - 4 \\ 2 + 4 \end{array}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{c} -2 \\ 6 \end{array}\right] $

This simplifies to:

$ \left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right] = \left[\begin{array}{c} -1 \\ 3 \end{array}\right] $

Therefore, the values of $ x $ and $ y $ are $ x = -1 $ and $ y = 3 $.

content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha\end{array}\right]$, then $A A^{\prime}$ is equal to
explanation:
Given the matrix $ A = \begin{bmatrix} \cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{bmatrix} $, we need to calculate the product $ A A^{\prime} $, where $ A^{\prime} $ is the transpose of matrix $ A $.

The transpose of $ A $, denoted $ A^{\prime} $, is:

$ A^{\prime} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{bmatrix} $

Now, we calculate the product $ A A^{\prime} $:

$ A A^{\prime} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{bmatrix} $

Performing the matrix multiplication, we have:

$ A A^{\prime} = \begin{bmatrix} (\cos \alpha)(\cos \alpha) + (\sin \alpha)(\sin \alpha) & (\cos \alpha)(-\sin \alpha) + (\sin \alpha)(\cos \alpha) \\ (-\sin \alpha)(\cos \alpha) + (\cos \alpha)(\sin \alpha) & (-\sin \alpha)(-\sin \alpha) + (\cos \alpha)(\cos \alpha) \end{bmatrix} $

Calculating each element:

Top left: $\cos^2 \alpha + \sin^2 \alpha = 1$ (using the Pythagorean identity)

Top right: $-\cos \alpha \sin \alpha + \sin \alpha \cos \alpha = 0$

Bottom left: $-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha + \cos \alpha \sin \alpha = 0$

Bottom right: $\sin^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \alpha = 1$ (using the Pythagorean identity)

Thus, the product is:

$ A A^{\prime} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $

This is the identity matrix $ I $. Hence,

$ A A^{\prime} = I $

content: The value of determinant $\left|\begin{array}{lll}a-b & b+c & a \\ b-a & c+a & b \\ c-a & a+b & c\end{array}\right|$ is
explanation:
To find the value of the determinant $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}a-b & b+c & a \\ b-a & c+a & b \\ c-a & a+b & c\end{array}\right|$, we can proceed with column and row operations to simplify it.

First, perform the column operation $C_3 \rightarrow C_2 + C_3$:

$ \Delta = \left|\begin{array}{ccc} a-b & b+c & a+b+c \\ b-a & c+a & a+b+c \\ c-a & a+b & a+b+c \end{array}\right| $

Factor out $(a+b+c)$, as it's common in the third column:

$ \Delta = (a+b+c) \left|\begin{array}{ccc} a-b & b+c & 1 \\ b-a & c+a & 1 \\ c-a & a+b & 1 \end{array}\right| $

Next, apply the row operations $R_2 \rightarrow R_2 - R_1$ and $R_3 \rightarrow R_3 - R_1$:

$ \Delta = (a+b+c) \left|\begin{array}{ccc} a-b & b+c & 1 \\ 2b & a-b & 0 \\ c-2a+b & a-c & 0 \end{array}\right| $

Since the last column after the row operations has two zeros, the determinant simplifies to:

$ \Delta = (a+b+c) \left( 1 \times \left( 2b(a-c) - (a-b)(c-2a+b) \right) \right) $

Now expand the expression inside the determinant:

$ = (a+b+c) \left[ 2ab - 2bc - (ac - 2a^2 + ab - bc + 2ab - b^2) \right] $

Simplify the terms:

$ = (a+b+c) \left[ 2ab - 2bc - ac + 2a^2 - ab + bc - 2ab + b^2 \right] $

Resulting in:

$ = (a+b+c) \left[ 2a^2 - ac - ab - bc + b^2 \right] $

content: If $\left(x_1, y_1\right),\left(x_2, y_2\right)$ and $\left(x_3, y_3\right)$ are the vertices of a triangle whose are is ' $k$ ' square units, then $\left|\begin{array}{lll}x_1 & y_1 & 4 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 4 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 4\end{array}\right|^2$ is
explanation:
To solve the problem, let's start by noting that the vertices of the triangle are given as $(x_1, y_1)$, $(x_2, y_2)$, and $(x_3, y_3)$ with a triangle area of $k$ square units.

The area $k$ of the triangle can be calculated using the determinant method as follows:

$ \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{array}\right| = k $

This implies:

$ \left|\begin{array}{lll} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{array}\right| = 2k $

Next, we need to evaluate the expression:

$ \left|\begin{array}{lll} x_1 & y_1 & 4 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 4 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 4 \end{array}\right|^2 $

This determinant can be rewritten and expanded using properties of determinants:

$ \left|\begin{array}{lll} x_1 & y_1 & 4 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 4 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 4 \end{array}\right| = 4 \times \left|\begin{array}{lll} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{array}\right| $

Thus, the determinant squared is:

$ \left(4 \cdot \left|\begin{array}{lll} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{array}\right|\right)^2 $

$ = 16 \left|\begin{array}{lll} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2 & y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{array}\right|^2 $

Substituting the value of the determinant in terms of $k$:

$ = 16 \cdot (2k)^2 = 16 \cdot 4k^2 = 64k^2 $

Therefore, the value of the expression is $64k^2$.

content: If a matrix $A$ is both symmetric and skew symmetric, then
explanation:
If a matrix $ A $ is both symmetric and skew-symmetric, let's analyze the implications:

A symmetric matrix satisfies the condition $ A^\prime = A $.

A skew-symmetric matrix satisfies the condition $ A^\prime = -A $.

For a matrix to be both symmetric and skew-symmetric, it must fulfill both conditions simultaneously:

$ A^\prime = A \quad \text{and} \quad A^\prime = -A $

Comparing these, we find:

$ A = -A $

This equation holds true only when every element of matrix $ A $ is zero, making $ A $ a zero matrix. Hence, if a matrix is both symmetric and skew-symmetric, it must be a zero matrix.
